videos.title: Top 10 Best Things to Do in Tunisia - Travel Video 2024
videos.description:
Are you planning a trip to Tunisia? Look no further! In this video, we have curated a comprehensive list of the best things to do in Tunisia, ensuring an unforgettable experience for you. Tunisia, located on the North African coast, boasts a rich history, vibrant culture, and breathtaking landscapes that will captivate your senses.

Discover the ancient ruins of Carthage, where you'll step back in time and witness the remains of a once-great civilization. Explore the glorious Medina of Tunis, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, where narrow alleyways and bustling markets offer an authentic taste of Tunisian daily life.

Unwind on the pristine beaches of Sidi Bou Said, a charming coastal town famous for its blue and white architecture. Indulge in a desert adventure, venturing into the Sahara Desert with a camel trek and spending a night under the starry sky. Don't forget to visit the stunning oasis of Tozeur, where palm groves and lush landscapes create a serene ambiance.

Immerse yourself in the culture by attending a traditional music performance or visiting one of the local souks to experience the vibrant colors and aromas of the Tunisian spices and handicrafts. And how can you miss out on the mouthwatering cuisine? Sample the delicious flavors of Tunisian dishes like couscous, brik, and the famous Tunisian pastries.

Whether you're a history enthusiast, beach lover, or adventure seeker, Tunisia has something for everyone. Join us on this virtual tour as we showcase the best attractions Tunisia has to offer!

videos.title: What I wish I knew before visiting TUNISIA | Ultimate Travel Guide 2026 - food, transport, safety
videos.description:
✨ subscribe ✨ & join the patreon for more https://www.patreon.com/tomthornton

book the same tour I did in Tunis: https://viator.tp.st/EjKGGiQZ

use the same travel e-seim (absolute lifesaver): https://airalo.tp.st/0a7CSKiV

I just got back from my one-week solo trip around Tunisia, which I planned and organised completely by myself.

It was a beautiful and surprising country and I absolutely loved my time there, but I also did learn a few things that I wish I knew before going, so I've compiled all of that wisdom in to the ultimate Tunisia travel guide for you, so that you can go and have the perfect vacation there from day one :)

I also feel really grateful to the people I met there and the kindness they showed me, so I want to do my part for helping to boost the tourism industry there. There's kind of a lack of good recent Tunisia information online, and I wanted to change that.

Tunisia is full of a wealth of historical and cultural sites, old markets and medinas, fortresses, roman ruins, and of course, sites in the southern part in the sahara, like tataouine, where star wars was filmed ;) And all of that adds up to a place that absolutely suits budget-friendly solo travel.



I went to Tunis, Sidi Bou Said, Carthage, Sfax, Sousse and El Jem while in Tunisia. It was an amazing tour, and I connected the dots with buses, louages, trains and taxis.

If you're planning your own solo trip to Tunisia, be sure to watch this video and ask me any specific questions in the comments.

videos.description:
Are you visiting Tunisia (تونس‎) for the first time?  Then this video will help you with what you shouldn’t do and conversely what you should do.

Wherever you’re going on your vacation to Tunisia, these Tunisian travel tips and pieces of advice will be invaluable in Tunis, Carthage, Sidi-Bou-Said, Hammamet, Sousse, Sfax, Tozeur, Djerba, The Sahara Desert or anywhere you may visit.

I hope this Tunisia travel guide helps you to both prepare and enjoy your holiday to this amazing country!

I'll tell you everything you need to know about:

00:00 Intro
00:22 Arriving
01:27 Culture
01:47 Language
02:39 Law
03:12 Money
03:16 Cash & Cards
03:50 ATMs / Cash Machines
04:51 Transport
05:01 Cars & Driving
06:34 Louages in depth
10:41 Trains
11:59 Buses & Coaches
12:21 Domestic Flights
12:36 Within Cities
12:40 Taxi
13:55 Buses
14:11 Tunis Light Rail
14:38 Food & Drink
14:40 Water & Tap Water
15:14 Tipping ("Baksheesh")
15:41 Shopping
15:43 Souks, Haggling
16:18 Antiques

This video is based on my personal experience at the time of traveling to Tunisia and your experience may differ.  Be sure to do your own research to get the latest information and most importantly keep an open mind.

videos.transcript: OK guys in this video I'm going to let you know what you shouldn't do and conversely what you should do and give you some general advice which is going to help you to have a way way better time when you come and visit Tunisia If you come here with an open mind but you know what you're doing you are going to have a much better time So that said let's get straight on to it Now when you arrive don't assume that you have to get a taxi from the airport, there is another way There's a couple of buses that run to the city center The SNT bus runs around every half an hour and then there's a slightly more expensive TUT which supposedly runs about every 15 minutes but most likely you are going to end up getting a taxi But don't expect a great welcome from these guys They'll probably refuse to use the meter You're going to have to negotiate up front and you're probably going to pay more than you should But do negotiate hard and don't put up with rubbish from these guys Personally I negotiated a upfront price with a taxi driver, he agreed to that and then during the ride he was trying to say it was more money, he kept trying to up the price I was firm and very very strong and would not give into it and at the end he got the agreed price and nothing more but do watch out for that, it's definitely a danger with those guys specifically but if you do get a negative first experience like that don't let it tinge your judgment of the country These guys are something special but Tunisians are really really great people and you're going to have a fantastic time once you get beyond that It's all up from here You shouldn't be surprised when local people go out of their way to help you expecting nothing in return I've seen such incredible friendliness and helpfulness here but of course at the same time don't assume everyone's like that There will be some people who are just after your money, they are the minority and it's really really easy to detect which one's which But about language, don't expect everyone to speak English here In the northern beach resorts you're a lot more likely to find people speaking English but when you're in other parts of the country and particularly the south you're going to find very very few The main language spoken here is Tunisian Arabic and there's also a lot of people speaking French, that's really really common place but you're going to have problems getting by in English in a lot of places in the country You're going to be heavily heavily reliant on Google translate and that really really does help, it's a lifesaver Now one little tip for you that I found If you're traveling around the country using local transport like the louages which I'll talk about in a lot more depth later Then it really really helps if you have your destination written in French and Arabic That way you can just show it at people to the ticket booths You can show it around to people if you're trying to find your minibus and it just kind of helps and those people will really help you back But of course never forget that when you come here you are traveling to a Muslim country and you should act accordingly Even in the less conservative north, Tunisian law still applies to personal relationships It's technically illegal for mixed gender couples to stay in the same room in a hotel if they're not married Now in actual fact this isn't really enforced by the hotels unless one or both of that couple are Tunisian but it's still worth being aware of and as a general rule I'd say if you're traveling as a couple it's best to use husband/wife rather than girlfriend/boyfriend it just helps to smooth things and make things a bit easier Now let's talk a little about money Don't assume that you can just get by with plastic Cash really is still king here and you really need to be carrying some around It's only in the bigger hotels and more tourist focused restaurants that you're going to find card payments accepted And don't forget to keep hold of some small notes and coins wherever you can It's really really helpful for the small purchases and also tips Take a little time to familiarize yourself with the coins It's really not obvious at first what's what You'll see here the 1 Dinar coin is actually very very similar to the 2 Dinar You can see here the two is just slightly bigger And when you do need to get some cash ATMs are readily available At least in the cities, towns and more tourist focused destinations You'll also find ATMs at the the airport of course before you leave But don't expect that you can find them everywhere If you're traveling around the country it really makes sense to be carrying some cash around with you At least enough to see you through until the next major stop Secondly don't expect to be able to withdraw big amounts There's plenty of ATMs I've seen that will only let you withdraw 300 Dinars That's less than €100, not exactly great when you're trying to limit the transaction fees Some will offer more, even up to 800 which seems to be about the biggest you can get Try the STB and BTL banks, they're about the best for this And thirdly in some of the smaller towns, don't expect to find the ATMs everywhere I've seen them often just clustered in the center and then you'll see pretty much none outside of there And lastly on ATMs, don't expect to be able to use your bank card I tried my Maestro card around here and couldn't get it to work anywhere My Mastercard was key, I assume Visa the same but you're probably going to need a Visa or Mastercard here OK now let's talk transport Unless you're getting some full guided tour around the country, you're going to need to know more than a little about how things work here Firstly if you decided to drive yourself, rent a car or whatever Don't assume it's going to be like driving in a western country Different rules apply here, or well don't as the case may be It's common place that traffic rules will be completely ignored Even sometimes in front of the police And talking about the police, don't forget to slow down and stop at the police checkpoints There's plenty around the and take your sedan rental car into the desert off road Unless you got a 4x4 and you know how to drive it off road, just don't Go with an experienced professional who knows what they're doing and has country It's effectively a shared minibus which does routes between cities, between towns and sometimes even rural routes But before I get into how it works, I just want to say don't be put off, it written in French and Arabic often times they'll just go and take you to where you need to go They're really really helpful, you're guaranteed you be where you need to be before long At some of the smaller stations you just go directly to the vehicle and pay there You never really know until you get to a particular station Just look around there, ask people if you need to and you'll find the lot more places, there'll be smaller towns maybe only serving a couple of destinations You need to know where to go and where to change if necessary So this one for example New Matmarta If you've come down to the Star Wars hotel and the sites of Matmarta then you'll end up here quite a way away, like a good 15 km from Old Matmata but this is the louage station, small There's just one there at the moment, you'd hardly know it's there and so in this case you buy a ticket in the little window behind there and then you seem to just wait and you'll be seen on when one appears And with these journeys it's all about knowing the regional hubs as well From New Matmarta here we're not going to get to any really big, long destinations What I got to do is change at Gabes, the biggest hub in the area Now it's not generally as important but could be useful to know There is generally a colour system for louages signified by a stripe running down the side of the vehicle The red stripe mini buses louages they'll stick two people on that front seat, but you can clearly see when there's a space for two Now it really is the best seat when there's just space for one there If you get one of those it's a real catch, if you just happen to turn up first and claim it first But there's more than just louage here, don't think it's the only way to get around There's also a train network here, although the trains aren't really the best The network is kind of infrequent, a little bit slow, prone to delays and not really very well maintained I took one train train from Tunis to Sousse at the start of my trip and after that I did just switch to louages But in doing so, aside from the experience of doing it I actually learned quite a lot about the trains just from talking to a local while we were waiting for the delayed train Now first class is available and that's what I opted for, but don't expect it to be what you would expect of first class It's very badly maintained, there's no air conditioning, it's pretty rough really but what I will say is the seats are actually dated, but they're pretty comfortable However, on your ticket there'll be a carriage number and there'll be a seat number assigned but don't expect that to actually work In practice what you'll find is the carriages aren't actually marked with a number and often the seats aren't marked anyway and even if they were, everyone just ignores it The normal system is that everyone just rushes on People will be pushing you out the way to try and get on the train and get a seat first I even saw people getting on the train while it was still coming in, before it even stopped So expect that, you're going to need to get on there quick to claim your seat Don't think that it's just going to be waiting for you Another option is the bus network, all the major cities have at least one bus station and it can be quite a comfortable way to travel Often on the longer journeys there will actually be aircon for a start But what you miss with the buses is the flexibility of the louages You do have to be there of course at a set time and with the buses it's really best to just buy your ticket at least a day in advance Flying domestically is also an option, although not one I chose But there is airports all over the country, just don't expect them to service the route that you might need every day Just check the flights using the normal methods online and the normal websites, see what you can find Now let's talk about getting around within towns and cities Now taxi is really going to be your primary way and taxis here must use the meter but at the same time, don't assume they always will You need to check in advance like in a lot of places Make sure they're willing to use the meter, if they're not, don't be afraid of just moving on In my experience you'll generally find an honest taxi driver just down the road who is willing to You can always flag down an empty taxi on the road and also sometimes you can just get in a shared taxi when there's someone else going the same direction as you Can be quite handy in some places Now another little thing to note about taxis regarding ripoffs and that kind of thing, is that a luggage surcharge is actually a normal thing That's actually fair, that's not a ripoff if you get asked for that, that's just the norm But the most useful thing I found there is that in cities like Tunis and Sousse the taxis can be really really busy and it can be an absolute pain trying to actually get one There can be multiple people waiting so it can be a real fight just to get a taxi Now if you're using Bolt, you don't have that problem of course You book one, you wait for it, you've got the number and that's it, it really simplifies things there You do pay a little bit more though, there's definitely a surcharge It would be cheaper just flagging one down, but it's well worth it for the convenience just using the app Now I will mention there is actually buses in the towns and cities, but the infrastructure really isn't set up for tourists so I wouldn't advise using them, there'll be no information on the destinations so you really won't know where you're going Unless you really know what you're doing or you're with a local, it's not worth it Now Tunis is a little unique in that it actually has a light rail system which is kind of handy The Metro is an above ground light rail system and it's actually kind of useful coz it serves a couple of the big hitter tourist sites like Carthage and Sidi-Bou-Said Purchase a ticket before you get on the metro at little booths that around the metro station and again it's really useful just to have the destination written in French and Arabic And that's really it for transportation Moving on to a completely different but often asked question, can you drink the tap water? Well, this is a kind of weird one and I would say don't believe everything you read online Because there seems to be completely different opinions Some people say it's fine Other people say, NO WAY! Now what I found from practical experience is that it really really doesn't taste very nice I know that just from cleaning my teeth Also wherever you go you're offered bottle water and that speaks volumes So I would say probably veer towards not drinking the tap water here And another common question of course that we all need to know, what's the rules regarding tipping? Well don't forget to tip here, it really is expected It's a common part of the culture here in North Africa If you're too tight with the bakshish as it's called here then you'll be seen as tight, even arrogant and tipping it's pretty much expected even for the smallest service so as I say earlier it's really handy just to carry some small change with your always for these sort of things Now let's move on to shopping Now don't forget that as a tourist at least you are going to need to haggle in a souk or medina The price that you're offered is going to be a multitude higher of what it should be and they'll know just from looking at you that you won't know the real price that it should be So you're going to end up paying more than a local would, of course you are but haggling is going to bring it down to a reasonable price and don't forget with these things the key is that both parties walk away happy So just have fun with it, but don't assume you can haggle everywhere You're not going to have much luck if you try and do it down a high street shop or in a supermarket Now smuggling of antiquities is a problem here which is enforced quite strongly If you're caught traveling outside of the country with antiquities then you could be arrested Make very sure that if you purchase something antique you have the right permission, you have the right paperwork before you leave That's going to save you a lot of pain And that's pretty much all the advice I have to offer you on Tunisia This an amazing country, there's so much to see and there's incredible friendly welcoming people You're going to have a great time here Just come prepared, know what you're doing and enjoy your trip I hope to see you next time guys

In 2013, I started my solo travel journey, leaving Indonesia in 2016 to become a full-time traveller and YouTube creator. My travels kicked off with a five-month trip across Asia, after which I moved to China in January 2017 to work as an English teacher, my first time living abroad. I left China in 2018 to fully commit to creating videos on YouTube, where I share my travels and the cultures I encounter.

Living nomadically since 2016, I settled in England in early 2022 after marrying my British husband, David. Now based in the UK, I continue to travel and share my adventures on YouTube.
